title: Cayley 2026 Problem 17
statement: Let $s$ be the sum of the digits of a positive two-digit integer $n$. Which of the following can be a value of $n-s$?
solution:
If $n$ has tens digit $x$ and ones digit $y$, then
$$n=10x+y,\qquad s=x+y.$$ 
So
$$n-s=(10x+y)-(x+y)=9x,$$
which must be a positive multiple of $9$. Among the choices, only $54$ works.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 18
statement: A grasshopper starts at $(0,0)$ and makes exactly $6$ unit jumps, each either up, down, left, or right. It can end at $N$ different points. What is $N$?
solution:
After $6$ moves, the parity of $x+y$ must be even. Also the distance condition gives $|x|+|y|\le 6$. The possible values of $|x|+|y|$ are $0,2,4,6$.

For $|x|+|y|=6$, there are $24$ points. For $|x|+|y|=4$, there are $16$ points. For $|x|+|y|=2$, there are $8$ points. For $|x|+|y|=0$, there is $1$ point. So
$$N=24+16+8+1=49.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 19
statement: Amira rolls a standard six-sided die exactly six times. The mean of her first three rolls is $3$. Her last three rolls are $a,b,c$. How many ordered triples $(a,b,c)$ make the mean of all six rolls an integer?
solution:
The first three rolls sum to $9$. So the total mean is an integer exactly when
$$\frac{9+a+b+c}{6}$$
is an integer, that is, when $9+a+b+c$ is a multiple of $6$. Since $a,b,c\in\{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$, the possible values of $a+b+c$ are from $3$ to $18$. We need
$$a+b+c=3,9,15.$$ 
These contribute $1$, $25$, and $10$ ordered triples respectively, for a total of
$$1+25+10=36.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 20
statement: All $5$-letter strings using only $A,B,C,D,E$ are listed in alphabetical order from $AAAAA$ to $EEEEE$. How many times does the letter $B$ occur in the $2026$th string?
solution: There are $5^5=3125$ strings. Each first letter appears in blocks of $5^4=625$, so the $2026$th string starts with $D$. Within the $D$-block, each second letter appears in blocks of $5^3=125$, so it starts with $DB$. Within the $DB$-block, each third letter appears in blocks of $25$, and the $2026$th string is the first string after $DBAEE$, namely $DBBAA$. That string contains exactly $2$ B's.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 21
statement: Four circles of radius $8$ are arranged so that each touches two others, and their centres are the vertices of a square. To the nearest integer, find the area of the shaded region enclosed by the four circles.

statementImages.alt: Add the official four-circle shaded-center diagram for Cayley 2026 Problem 21.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution:
The centres form a $16\times 16$ square. Inside that square, the circles cut out four quarter-circles of radius $8$, whose total area equals one full circle of radius $8$. So the shaded area is
$$16\cdot 16-\pi(8)^2=256-64\pi\approx 54.94.$$ 
Rounded to the nearest integer, this is $55$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 22
statement: Of $100$ movie-watchers, $47$ bought popcorn, more than $40$ bought a drink, $x$ bought both, and $2x$ bought neither. If $x>0$, what is the largest possible value of $x$?
solution:
Let $y$ be the number who bought a drink. Then the four disjoint groups are:
- popcorn only: $47-x$
- drink only: $y-x$
- both: $x$
- neither: $2x$

Their total is
$$100=(47-x)+(y-x)+x+2x=47+y+x.$$ 
So
$$y=53-x.$$ 
Since more than $40$ bought a drink, we need
$$53-x>40,$$
so
$$x<13.$$ 
Thus the largest possible integer value is $12$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 23
statement:
For distinct positive integers $p,q,r$, the four numbers
$$2r-21,\quad 3r-1,\quad r+12,\quad 3r-17$$
are equal to $p,2p,q,2q$ in some order. Find the sum of all possible values of $r$.
solution:
Since the four given numbers are exactly $p,2p,q,2q$ in some order, their sum is
$$3p+3q.$$ 
So
$$(2r-21)+(3r-1)+(r+12)+(3r-17)=3p+3q,$$
which simplifies to
$$9r-27=3p+3q,$$
hence
$$p+q=3r-9.$$ 
Now try the possible assignments of two of the four expressions to be $p$ and $q$. The only valid cases are:
$$p=3r-17,\quad 2p=2r-21 \Rightarrow 6r-34=2r-21 \Rightarrow r=41,$$
and
$$p=2r-21,\quad 2p=3r-17 \Rightarrow 4r-42=3r-17 \Rightarrow r=25.$$ 
Thus the required sum is
$$41+25=66.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 24
statement: In the shown $6\times 6\times 6$ cube, points $C$ and $F$ are vertices, and $A,B,D,E$ are midpoints of their edges. Lines $AF$ and $EC$ meet at $G$, and $BF$ and $DC$ meet at $H$. Pieces are sliced off by the planes $ABF$ and $EGHD$, and the same process is repeated on each of the four vertical edges. If the volume of the remaining solid is $V$, what are the rightmost two digits of $V$?

statementImages.alt: Add the official 6 by 6 by 6 cube slicing diagram for Cayley 2026 Problem 24.
statementImages.caption: Diagram from the official contest.

solution:
Let
$$W=[EFDC],$$
the volume of pyramid $EFDC$. Since $EF=DF=3$ and $CF=6$,
$$W=\frac{1}{3}\cdot \frac{1}{2}\cdot 3\cdot 3\cdot 6=9.$$ 
The inner pyramid removed from this pyramid is similar in each linear dimension by a factor of $\frac12$, so its volume is $\frac18W$. Thus the remaining sliced piece from one corner has volume
$$W-\frac18W=\frac78W.$$ 
Two such pieces are removed from each vertical-edge section, so each section loses
$$2\cdot \frac78W=\frac74W.$$ 
There are $4$ congruent sections, so the total removed volume is
$$4\cdot \frac74W=7W=63.$$ 
The whole cube has volume
$$6^3=216,$$
so the remaining volume is
$$216-63=153.$$ 
The rightmost two digits are $53$.
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo

title: Cayley 2026 Problem 25
statement: The integers from $1$ to $2k$ are written in order. Arthur erases $k$ consecutive integers. The remaining integers have sum $819$. Find the sum of all possible values of $k$.
solution:
The sum of $1+2+\cdots+2k$ is
$$\frac{2k(2k+1)}{2}=2k^2+k.$$ 
Suppose the erased block starts at $m$ and has length $k$. Then its sum is
$$m+(m+1)+\cdots+(m+k-1)=km+\frac{(k-1)k}{2}.$$ 
So the remaining sum is
$$2k^2+k-\left(km+\frac{(k-1)k}{2}\right)=819.$$ 
This simplifies to
$$k(3k-2m+3)=1638.$$ 
Checking divisor pairs of $1638$ that can occur in this form gives exactly two valid values:
$$k=26\quad\text{and}\quad k=39.$$ 
Their sum is
$$26+39=65.$$
credit: 2026 Cayley Contest, CEMC, University of Waterloo
